Joshua stands before all Israel at Shechem with something to say that cannot wait. This is the voice of a man preparing to leave the world, and every word carries weight. He reviews the entire history of God's redemption—not to recite a comfortable story, but to confront his people with an unavoidable reality: everything they possess, they received from the hand of God. Abraham was chosen from idolatry. The exodus happened by divine power, not human strategy. The conquest succeeded through God's direct intervention—hornets driving out kings, walls falling flat, enemies delivered into their hands without them lifting a sword. Joshua does not soften this truth with sentiment. He wants Israel to see exactly what they owe and to whom. The weight of grace, properly understood, is not comforting—it is binding.

Then Joshua turns the mirror around and forces the people to choose. This moment is not a ceremony of affirmation where everyone nods together. Joshua tells them plainly: God is holy and jealous. He will not tolerate rebellion. If you turn to other gods, He will bring disaster upon you. These are not gentle words of encouragement. Joshua is saying that covenant with the living God is not a comfortable arrangement—it is a binding obligation with consequences. He even declares that the people are not capable of serving God as they should. This statement shocks us. Why would he discourage them from making a covenant? Because Joshua knows something vital: the commitment they are about to make will be tested. The flesh is weak. Prosperity breeds forgetfulness. The generation that had seen God's wonders would pass away, and their children would face the subtle seduction of other gods. Joshua is stripping away their false confidence and forcing them to understand what they are actually choosing.

The people respond with enthusiasm, and Joshua does not accept their first answer. He presses them again. This is not a negotiation or a marketing pitch. He establishes a covenant, writes it in the Book of the Law, and sets up a stone as a witness. That stone is not a monument to their faithfulness. It is a stone of testimony against them. Joshua's own words are stark: this stone "will be a witness against you if you ever deny your God." Joshua knows what is coming. Within a generation, Israel will turn to idolatry despite everything they have witnessed. The stone would stand as silent proof that they knew better. This is the covenant God makes with His people—not a contract that fails when they fail, but an unbreakable agreement that includes judgment when they break faith. As you read this chapter, understand that every covenant God makes with us has teeth. His grace is real and His redemption is certain, but His expectations are absolute. The question Joshua puts to ancient Israel echoes still: Will you serve the LORD in sincerity and truth, or will you turn away? The choice is yours, but the consequences belong to God.
